title: The recipe that was never written down
summary: In a kitchen in Lisbon, a granddaughter tries to write down her grandmother's bean stew before it is lost, and the two of them discover that the recipe is not really about quantities at all.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: Avó, please sit down. I have a notebook and a pen. I want to write the feijoada down properly this time, before — well, before I forget how you make it.

body.speaker: Avó Lurdes
body.text: Before I die, you mean. You can say it. I am eighty-one, not made of glass. But you will be wasting your pen. There is nothing to write.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: There must be. How many beans? How much chouriço? You have made this every Sunday for sixty years.

body.speaker: Avó Lurdes
body.text: How many beans? Enough. How much chouriço? Until it smells right. I have never measured a single thing in this kitchen. My hands measure. Your notebook cannot hold what my hands know.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: That is exactly the problem. When your hands are gone, the recipe is gone. I am trying to keep it.

body.speaker: Avó Lurdes
body.text: I understand. And it is kind. But listen — I did not learn this from a notebook. I learned it standing where you are standing, watching my mother, getting it wrong for ten years. The recipe is not the words. The recipe is the ten years.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: So you are saying I should give up writing it and just... cook it badly for ten years?

body.speaker: Avó Lurdes
body.text: I am saying the notebook will save the wrong thing. Write 'four hundred grams of beans' and you will make a stew that is correct and dead. The day you add too much salt, taste it, and feel your stomach drop — that is the day you begin to learn. The mistake teaches. The recipe only reminds.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: Then why do cookbooks exist at all?

body.speaker: Avó Lurdes
body.text: For people who have already cooked, mostly. A cookbook is a note from one cook to another who already understands. To you, today, 'cook until tender' means nothing. To me it means a whole afternoon. The words are the same. What is behind them is not.

body.speaker: Inês
body.text: All right. I will make you a deal, Avó. I will keep the notebook — but only to write what you say while we cook, not instead of cooking. And every Sunday I stand here with you until I can do it with my eyes closed.

body.speaker: Avó Lurdes
body.text: Now that I will agree to. Bring the notebook if it comforts you. But put it down when I tell you to put it down, and put your hands in the pot. The first lesson is the smell. Close your eyes. No — not the beans yet. Smell the onions. When they go from sharp to sweet, that is the moment. There is no number for that moment. There never was.

annotations.meaningVi: feijoada — món hầm đậu của Bồ Đào Nha
chouriço — xúc xích Bồ Đào Nha
không mỏng manh dễ vỡ (ý: đừng nâng niu quá)
đến khi nó dậy mùi đúng
đong đo một thứ nào đó
đôi tay tôi đong đo (thay vì cân đo)

annotations.phrase: getting it wrong for ten years

annotations.meaningVi: từ bỏ việc ghi nó lại
nhắm mắt cũng làm được (thành thạo)
nếu nó làm cháu yên tâm

annotations.phrase: There is no number for that moment
annotations.meaningVi: không có con số nào cho khoảnh khắc đó

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life experience + 'until' / 'when' time clauses
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại hoàn thành chỉ kinh nghiệm cả đời: 'You have made this every Sunday for sixty years.' / 'I have never measured a single thing in this kitchen.' Bài cũng dùng mệnh đề thời gian với 'until' và 'when' (dùng thì hiện tại để chỉ tương lai): 'cook until it smells right', 'When they go from sharp to sweet, that is the moment.' — sau 'when/until' không dùng 'will'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The text uses the present perfect for lifelong experience: 'You have made this every Sunday for sixty years.' / 'I have never measured a single thing in this kitchen.' It also uses time clauses with 'until' and 'when' (present tense for future meaning): 'cook until it smells right', 'When they go from sharp to sweet, that is the moment.' — no 'will' after 'when/until'.

translationVi:
Inês: Bà ơi, bà ngồi xuống đi. Cháu có quyển sổ và cây bút. Lần này cháu muốn ghi món feijoada cho đàng hoàng, trước khi — à, trước khi cháu quên cách bà nấu.

Avó Lurdes: Trước khi bà chết, ý cháu là vậy. Cháu cứ nói thẳng. Bà tám mươi mốt, đâu phải làm bằng thủy tinh. Nhưng cháu sẽ phí cây bút thôi. Chẳng có gì để ghi cả.

Inês: Phải có chứ. Bao nhiêu đậu? Bao nhiêu chouriço? Bà nấu món này mỗi Chủ Nhật suốt sáu mươi năm.

Avó Lurdes: Bao nhiêu đậu? Đủ. Bao nhiêu chouriço? Đến khi dậy mùi đúng. Bà chưa bao giờ đong đo một thứ gì trong bếp này. Tay bà đong đo. Quyển sổ của cháu không chứa nổi điều tay bà biết.

Inês: Đó chính là vấn đề. Khi tay bà không còn, công thức cũng mất. Cháu đang cố giữ nó.

Avó Lurdes: Bà hiểu. Và như vậy là tử tế. Nhưng nghe này — bà không học món này từ quyển sổ. Bà học bằng cách đứng đúng chỗ cháu đang đứng, nhìn mẹ bà, và làm sai suốt mười năm. Công thức không phải là chữ. Công thức là mười năm đó.

Inês: Vậy bà bảo cháu nên bỏ việc ghi chép và chỉ... nấu dở suốt mười năm?

Avó Lurdes: Bà bảo quyển sổ sẽ lưu nhầm thứ. Viết 'bốn trăm gram đậu' rồi cháu sẽ nấu ra một nồi hầm đúng mà vô hồn. Ngày cháu cho quá nhiều muối, nếm thử, và thấy thót ruột — đó là ngày cháu bắt đầu học. Lỗi lầm dạy ta. Công thức chỉ nhắc ta.

Inês: Vậy tại sao sách dạy nấu ăn lại tồn tại?

Avó Lurdes: Chủ yếu cho những người đã từng nấu. Sách nấu ăn là lời nhắn từ đầu bếp này tới đầu bếp khác đã hiểu rồi. Với cháu, hôm nay, 'nấu đến khi mềm' chẳng nghĩa gì. Với bà nó nghĩa là cả một buổi chiều. Chữ thì giống nhau. Thứ đằng sau chúng thì không.

Inês: Được rồi. Cháu thỏa thuận với bà nhé. Cháu sẽ giữ quyển sổ — nhưng chỉ để ghi những gì bà nói trong lúc nấu, không phải thay cho việc nấu. Và mỗi Chủ Nhật cháu đứng đây với bà cho tới khi nhắm mắt cũng làm được.

Avó Lurdes: Vậy thì bà đồng ý. Mang sổ theo nếu nó làm cháu yên tâm. Nhưng đặt nó xuống khi bà bảo đặt xuống, và cho tay vào nồi. Bài học đầu tiên là mùi. Nhắm mắt lại. Không — chưa cho đậu vội. Ngửi mùi hành. Khi nó chuyển từ hăng sang ngọt, đó là khoảnh khắc. Không có con số nào cho khoảnh khắc đó. Chưa bao giờ có.

questions.prompt: Why does Inês want to write down the feijoada?
options: So the recipe is not lost when her grandmother is gone
Because the dish is too salty
questions.explanation: 'before I forget how you make it' / 'When your hands are gone, the recipe is gone.'

questions.prompt: How does Avó Lurdes answer 'how many beans?'
options: She does not know the dish
questions.explanation: 'How many beans? Enough... I have never measured a single thing in this kitchen.'

questions.prompt: How did Avó Lurdes learn the dish?
options: By watching her mother and getting it wrong for ten years
questions.explanation: 'watching my mother, getting it wrong for ten years.'

questions.prompt: According to the grandmother, what is the recipe really?
options: The ten years, not the words
A family secret kept on paper
questions.explanation: 'The recipe is not the words. The recipe is the ten years.'

questions.prompt: What does she say a notebook with '400 grams of beans' produces?
options: A stew that is correct and dead
questions.explanation: 'you will make a stew that is correct and dead.'

questions.prompt: When does she say real learning begins?
options: The day you add too much salt, taste it, and feel your stomach drop
The day you buy a cookbook
The first day in the kitchen
questions.explanation: 'feel your stomach drop — that is the day you begin to learn. The mistake teaches.'

questions.prompt: Who are cookbooks really for, in her view?
options: People who have already cooked and understand what the words mean
questions.explanation: 'A cookbook is a note from one cook to another who already understands.'

questions.prompt: What does 'cook until tender' mean to each of them?
options: Nothing to Inês today; a whole afternoon to the grandmother
questions.explanation: 'To you, today, "cook until tender" means nothing. To me it means a whole afternoon.'

options: Keep the notebook only to record what is said while they cook, and stand with her grandmother every Sunday until she masters it
questions.explanation: 'I will keep the notebook — but only to write what you say while we cook... every Sunday I stand here with you.'

questions.prompt: What is the first lesson the grandmother gives?
options: The smell — to notice when the onions go from sharp to sweet
questions.explanation: 'The first lesson is the smell... When they go from sharp to sweet, that is the moment.'

questions.prompt: What does 'not made of glass' tell us about the grandmother?
options: She is not fragile and does not want to be treated delicately
questions.explanation: 'I am eighty-one, not made of glass.' — she can handle plain talk.

options: Some knowledge lives in the hands and the years of mistakes that built it; writing the recipe down can record the words but cannot, by itself, pass on the real skill.
Older people refuse to share recipes.
Bean stew is impossible to learn.
questions.explanation: The dialogue contrasts written quantities with embodied, mistake-earned knowledge.

cloze.template:
Inês: Avó, please sit down. I have a notebook and a pen. I want to write the feijoada down properly this time, before — well, before I forget how you make it.
Avó Lurdes: Before I die, you mean. You can say it. I am eighty-one, not made of {{b1}}. But you will be wasting your pen. There is nothing to write.
Inês: There must be. How many beans? How much chouriço? You have made this every Sunday for sixty years.
Avó Lurdes: How many beans? Enough. How much chouriço? Until it smells right. I have never {{b2}} a single thing in this kitchen. My hands measure. Your notebook cannot hold what my hands know.
Inês: That is exactly the problem. When your hands are gone, the recipe is gone. I am trying to keep it.
Avó Lurdes: I understand. And it is kind. But listen — I did not learn this from a notebook. I learned it standing where you are standing, watching my mother, getting it {{b3}} for ten years. The recipe is not the words. The recipe is the ten {{b4}}.
Inês: So you are saying I should give up writing it and just... cook it badly for ten years?
Avó Lurdes: I am saying the notebook will save the wrong thing. Write 'four hundred grams of beans' and you will make a stew that is correct and {{b5}}. The day you add too much salt, taste it, and feel your {{b6}} drop — that is the day you begin to learn. The mistake {{b7}}. The recipe only reminds.
Inês: Then why do cookbooks exist at all?
Avó Lurdes: For people who have already cooked, mostly. A cookbook is a note from one cook to another who already {{b8}}. To you, today, 'cook until tender' means nothing. To me it means a whole afternoon. The words are the same. What is behind them is not.
Inês: All right. I will make you a deal, Avó. I will keep the notebook — but only to write what you say while we cook, not instead of cooking. And every Sunday I stand here with you until I can do it with my eyes {{b9}}.
Avó Lurdes: Now that I will agree to. Bring the notebook if it comforts you. But put it down when I tell you to put it down, and put your hands in the pot. The first lesson is the {{b10}}. Close your eyes. No — not the beans yet. Smell the onions. When they go from sharp to {{b11}}, that is the moment. There is no number for that moment. There never was.

blanks.explanation: Getting it wrong for ten years.
The recipe is the ten years.
Do it with my eyes closed.
The first lesson is the smell.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The grandmother believes the mistake teaches and the recipe only reminds. Think of something you can do 'with your eyes closed' — could you actually write down everything you know about it, or is part of it stored only in your hands?
